original_abstract: The practical implementation of aqueous zinc metal batteries is severely limited by persistent water-based corrosion, driven by the continuous transport of reactive water molecules to the electrode interface within the zinc-ion solvation shell. To address this, we introduce a sacrificial solvation strategy designed to exclude active water from the interface while facilitating the in situ formation of a robust, fluoride-rich solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) with an organic-inorganic gradient structure. By leveraging the simultaneous decomposition of a methanol additive and Zn(CF₃SO₃)₂, we engineered an SEI characterized by an organic-rich exterior (containing CH₂OC- and C₅ species) for structural flexibility and an inorganic-rich (ZnF₂) interior for rapid ion diffusion. This tailored interface enables exceptionally stable, corrosion-free cycling, achieving a 99.5% average Coulombic efficiency over 300 cycles on copper substrates. Notably, Zn//Zn symmetric cells demonstrated a record cumulative plating capacity of 10 Ah/cm² at a high current density of 40 mA/cm². Furthermore, full cells pairing a VO₂ cathode with an ultra-thin (20 μm) zinc anode—operating at a low N/P ratio of 2 and a high areal capacity of 4.7 mAh/cm²—exhibited stable performance for over 250 cycles. By achieving commercial-level capacities with thin zinc foils, this work simplifies the manufacturing pipeline and provides a clear pathway for the integration of zinc batteries into grid-scale stationary energy storage.
humanized_abstract: The commercialization of aqueous zinc metal batteries is impeded by the persistent water-based corrosion issue, which is attributed to the dynamic transportation of the reactive water molecules to the electrode interface within the zinc-ion solvation shell. Here, we propose a sacrificial solvation approach to remove the harmful water molecules from the electrode interface and produce a fluoride-based solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) by in situ decomposition of methanol and Zn(CF₃SO₃)₂. The resulting SEI is composed of an organic-inorganic gradient structure with an organic-rich outer layer (including CH₂OC- and C₅ species) for elasticity and an inorganic-rich inner layer of ZnF₂ for fast ion diffusion. With this SEI effectively prevents the corrosion and achieves a 99.5% average Coulombic efficiency for 300 cycles on copper substrates. Meanwhile, the Zn//Zn symmetric cells yield a record cumulative plating capacity of 10 Ah/cm² at the current density of 40 mA/cm². In addition, full cells made of VO₂ cathode material and an ultra-thin zinc foil anode (thickness: 20 μm) with low N/P ratio of 2 and an areal capacity of 4.7 mAh/cm² give a stable performance over 250 cycles. The commercial-level capacities obtained from the thin zinc foils by simplifying the fabrication process are of great value for the large-scale application of zinc batteries in grid-scale stationary energy storage.
